name: Jordyn Tyson
blurb: There’s a real chance the ZAP Model is too bullish on Jordyn Tyson — but within the predictive analytics we care about, he looks the part. Outside of draft capital, Breakout Score carries the most weight in the wide receiver ZAP Model by a wide margin. Tyson’s mark checks in at 95.1, the best in this year’s class and good for the 99th percentile. That’s largely because he produced at a high level throughout his collegiate career. As a young freshman at Colorado, Tyson led the team in receiving i

name: Carnell Tate
blurb: We’ve seen Chris Olave, Garrett Wilson, Jaxon Smith-Njigba, Marvin Harrison Jr., and Emeka Egbuka go in Round 1 out of Ohio State over the last five years. Now we can add Carnell Tate to that list. Tate went fourth overall to Tennessee, and while that draft capital is as strong as any Buckeye receiver in this run, his ZAP score ranks second-lowest among them, ahead of only Egbuka. That’s not necessarily a knock — his 65.3 Breakout Score sits in the 87th percentile — it’s just that the others bro

name: Makai Lemon
blurb: The Amon-Ra St. Brown comp for Makai Lemon may be a little played out, but it’s still a quick way to frame his ceiling in fantasy. Both are technicians with elite short- area quickness and a relentless edge. Like St. Brown, Lemon has that dog in him. And like St. Brown, Lemon lived in the slot in college. His 75.8% career slot rate ranks in the 88th percentile among ZAP Model wideouts since 2020. That’s rare territory for a first-rounder, too — only Jaylen Waddle, Kadarius Toney, Treylon Burks,

name: KC Concepcion
blurb: The fantasy football world probably won’t love KC Concepcion landing in Cleveland, but there were definitely worse spots. The Browns have a beatable depth chart, even with Denzel Boston, so Concepcion could realistically lead the team in target share as early as this season. And new head coach Todd Monken is coming off a three-year run as Baltimore’s offensive coordinator, where he helped Zay Flowers produce multiple fantasy-relevant campaigns. Flowers, like Concepcion, was a versatile playmaker

name: Omar Cooper
blurb: The best tackle-breaker in this wide receiver class is probably Omar Cooper. He avoided an absurd 0.31 tackles per reception across his career, a 97th percentile mark among ZAP Model wideouts since 2020. His efficiency profile is solid, too. Cooper posted a 79th percentile career yards per route run and an 82nd percentile first downs per route run. He split time between the slot and perimeter — a 48% career slot rate, per PFF — but was similarly effective in both alignments. The analytical conce

name: Germie Bernard
blurb: Pittsburgh has needed a reliable slot option, and they got one in Alabama’s Germie Bernard. Honestly, it’s one of the most Steelers picks imaginable. Bernard is tough. He’s a solid 206 pounds and forced missed tackles at an 84th percentile rate across his collegiate career. Coaches moved him around, too — he logged 36 rush attempts for 184 yards, including an 18-carry, 101-yard, 2-touchdown performance in 2025 at Alabama. Bernard dealt with strong competition at every stop of his career, which p

name: Denzel Boston
blurb: The expectation was that Denzel Boston would be a first-round pick. He didn’t get that top-32 capital, but he still landed early in Round 2 with Cleveland. Boston spent all four years at Washington, sharing the field with Rome Odunze, Jalen McMillan, and Ja’Lynn Polk during his first two seasons. Naturally, his production was limited during that stretch. Even accounting for that, his eventual breakout was a bit underwhelming. In 2024, Boston posted a 2.00 receiving yards per team pass attempt ra

name: De’Zhaun Stribling
blurb: In the pre-draft version of the prospect guide, De’Zhaun Stribling’s blurb read, “Unless draft capital surprises in a meaningful way, Stribling isn’t someone you’ll need to prioritize in rookie drafts.” Well, about that... Stribling going 33rd overall to San Francisco was one of the biggest surprises of the draft — at least among skill position players. Because his profile, quite frankly, doesn’t match that kind of capital. It may not even be a Day 2 profile. The analytics are underwhelming. He

name: Ja’Kobi Lane
blurb: Ja’Kobi Lane is one of just five early-declare wide receivers in this year’s class, spending all three collegiate seasons at USC. After catching just seven passes in six games as a freshman, he jumped to 43 and then 49 receptions over the next two years. The production profile, though, is underwhelming. His 2024 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt was just 1.14, rising to 2.35 as a junior. Even that peak ranked outside the top 20 in the class. The result is a 35.9 Breakout Score — a b

name: Antonio Williams
blurb: In an underwhelming rookie class, Antonio Williams is at least somewhat interesting. Williams spent his entire collegiate career at Clemson and produced right away. As a freshman, he caught 56 passes for 604 yards and 4 touchdowns, leading the team in receiving. Clemson was fairly pass-heavy that season, though, so his 1.26 receiving yards per team pass attempt wasn’t especially notable. Injuries limited him to five games as a sophomore. Then, over his final two seasons, Williams never exceeded

name: Zachariah Branch
blurb: Back in March, I thought Zachariah Branch might be the player I’d fade most in rookie drafts this year. But given how this class shook out, he’s almost...interesting. There are some positives. Branch is an early declare, and the model gives him credit for his versatility and unique usage. He’s explosive, shifty, and can operate in short areas. The negatives? There are plenty. Branch never truly produced at a high level. His raw receiving yards per team pass attempt failed to reach 2.0 in a seaso

name: Caleb Douglas
blurb: Entering the draft, Miami’s wide receiver depth chart was arguably the weakest in the league. Taking Caleb Douglas in Round 3 didn’t do much to change that. Maybe Douglas proves the ZAP Model wrong, but there’s not a lot to like here. He was projected to go outside the top 200, according to NFL Mock Draft Database, and the underlying metrics reflect that. Douglas posted a 25th percentile Breakout Score, a 28th percentile career yards per route run, a 15th percentile best-season yards per route r

name: Malachi Fields
blurb: Malachi Fields’ production profile is lacking. He began his collegiate career at Virginia in 2021, catching just 11 passes as a true freshman before a foot injury limited him to one game as a sophomore. His junior season showed some promise — 58 receptions for 811 yards and 5 touchdowns — resulting in a 2.24 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark. That ended up being his best year. Fields played two more seasons — one at Virginia and one at Notre Dame — but neither produced a strong

name: Chris Brazzell
blurb: Chris Brazzell slipped a bit on draft weekend, landing with Carolina at Pick 83. As a second-round prospect, there were questions. As a late Day 2 pick? The upside becomes easier to chase. Brazzell played in Tennessee’s offense under Josh Heupel, which hasn’t always translated cleanly to the ZAP Model. It’s burned us before, so there’s some inherent risk here. At 6’4”, 198 pounds, Brazzell has the prototypical vertical build. He used it to stretch defenses, too. Among receivers in this class, he

name: Ted Hurst
blurb: Ted Hurst gained a lot of steam during the pre-draft process, rising from a projected 180th overall pick in mid-January to a top-80 selection by April. He ultimately went 84th overall — a big win for a small-school prospect. Analytically, Hurst is tough to pin down. His Breakout Score sits around 42, which is basically average, but he’s a younger prospect (still under 22) with good length. He was also used aggressively downfield in college. His 28.6% career deep target rate ranks top- 10 in this

name: Chris Bell
blurb: Some respected draft analysts believe Chris Bell has first-round talent, and that a late-season ACL tear was the main reason he didn’t get that kind of capital. The film is enticing — he’s built like AJ Brown, and the upside is obvious. Seeing him fall to Pick 94 is at least a little concerning. That said, among the run of Round 3 wide receivers in this class, Bell offers one of the better analytical profiles. It’s not elite, but his 48.2 Breakout Score lands in the 68th percentile — and it tops

name: Zavion Thomas
blurb: Zavion Thomas was one of those picks that had me doing the confused Nick Young meme. I even mentioned him on a podcast recently as an interesting late-round dart throw in rookie drafts, but never in a million years did I expect Day 2 draft capital. The only reason he came up at all was because this class lacks clear later-round wide receiver targets. There are a couple of positives. Carnell Tate is the only receiver in this class younger than Thomas. He’s not an early declare, though — he spent

name: Elijah Sarratt
blurb: The Ravens took Ja’Kobi Lane on Day 2 and followed it up with Elijah Sarratt in Round 4. That’s later than his projected Round 2 to Round 3 capital, so his statistical comps may look a bit more optimistic than they should. There’s a real argument that Sarratt is the better prospect, though. After beginning his career at Saint Francis as a zero-star recruit, he finished with a 51.8 Breakout Score — a 71st percentile mark and one of the stronger figures in this class. That’s largely due to his con

name: Reggie Virgil
blurb: Reggie Virgil spent three seasons at Miami (OH) before transferring to Texas Tech in 2025. The early production was minimal — just two receptions across his first two years. As a junior, though, he flashed with a 2.20 receiving yards per team pass attempt mark, leading to a respectable 40.9 Breakout Score. The issue is that the production didn’t meaningfully improve after the move to the Big 12. Virgil has a lean frame and was used primarily as a vertical threat. Among the 46 Combine invitees, h

name: Skyler Bell
blurb: Skyler Bell played just one game as a freshman at Wisconsin, but broke out in Year 2 with 30 receptions for 444 yards and 5 touchdowns. After a dip as a junior, he transferred to UConn. The production improved, as expected. Over his two seasons with the Huskies, Bell posted prorated receiving yards per team pass attempt marks of 2.17 and 2.82. The issue is that UConn’s strength of schedule dragged those numbers down after adjustment, and because his peak came later in his career, his Breakout Sc

name: Brenen Thompson
blurb: The ZAP Model doesn’t love Brenen Thompson because he’s 164 pounds and had a Breakout Score of 35.4, a 41st percentile tally. In the ZAP Model database, the only sub- 180-pound wide receivers to post a 15-plus PPR points-per-game season within their first three years are first-rounders DeVonta Smith and Jordan Addison. Smaller receivers can still offer usable production — think Josh Downs or Darnell Mooney — but many of those mini-hits came with significantly stronger Breakout Scores than Thomps

name: Bryce Lance
blurb: The Saints took Jordyn Tyson in Round 1, followed it up with tight end Oscar Delp in Round 3, and then added Bryce Lance late in Round 4. From a fit standpoint, it makes sense. Lance is a big-bodied perimeter receiver who can stretch the field and complement their existing playmakers — if he develops. That’s a big “if.” Lance spent five years at North Dakota State, redshirting as a true freshman and mostly playing special teams over the next two seasons. Through 2023, he had just one career rece

name: Barion Brown
blurb: The Saints kept adding weapons in this draft, selecting Barion Brown in Round 6. He’s getting a profile update because the ZAP Model…oddly doesn’t mind him. Brown is fast — legitimately fast. A former track athlete, he was heavily involved as a returner across four seasons — three at Kentucky and one at LSU — and he backed up the speed with a 4.40 forty at the Combine. All things considered, the production profile isn’t terrible. At his size and with that speed, Brown likely projects as a gadget

name: Kevin Coleman
blurb: Given their depth chart, the Dolphins needed to add at wide receiver in this draft. They took two on Day 2 and then grabbed Kevin Coleman, a wideout who played for four different schools throughout his collegiate career, in Round 5. Coleman is a true slot receiver. Over 91% of his career routes came from the slot — the highest rate in this class. And it wasn’t scheme-driven. Across four different programs, he posted slot rates of at least 87% at every stop, per PFF. His best season came at Missi

name: Kaden Wetjen
blurb: According to NFL Mock Draft Database, the Steelers selected Kaden Wetjen about 100 picks earlier than expected. That’s kind of been the theme of this draft. They didn’t take Wetjen for traditional wide receiver production — or at least, they shouldn’t have. He caught just 23 passes across three seasons at Iowa, and more than 54% of his targets came behind the line of scrimmage. The goal was simple for his coaches in college: get Wetjen the ball in space and let him create. The production metrics

name: Colbie Young
blurb: Only six wide receivers in the ZAP Model database have averaged at least 14 PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons after being drafted outside the top 100: Allen Hurns, Martavis Bryant, Tyreek Hill, Hunter Renfrow, Amon-Ra St. Brown, and Puka Nacua. That’s rare air. And there’s no consistent size archetype among them. So while Colbie Young checking in at 6’5”, 218 pounds may have appealed to Cincinnati, it doesn’t really move the needle for fantasy. Young ends up as a Day 3 pick

name: Kendrick Law
blurb: The ZAP Model isn’t fond of Kendrick Law. He failed to register a Breakout Score, and he’s not expected to go early in the draft. But his profile is undeniably unique. Law spent three seasons at Alabama doing very little as a receiver — just 33 total catches and no meaningful efficiency spike. He transferred to Kentucky in 2025 and had his most productive year, catching 53 passes for 540 yards. That translated to a 1.47 receiving yards per team pass attempt mark, the fourth-worst top season in t

name: Emmanuel Henderson
blurb: Among the 46 wide receivers invited to this year’s NFL Combine, Emmanuel Henderson ranked second with a 40.5% career deep-ball target rate. He was also fourth in collegiate average depth of target. What’s interesting is that Henderson wasn’t strictly a boundary-only player. He ran about 36% of his routes from the slot, which is a fairly healthy rate for someone with that kind of downfield profile. Henderson’s college path took him to Alabama for three seasons before transferring to Kansas for hi

name: Eric McAlister
blurb: Texas Christian wide receivers haven’t exactly inspired confidence lately. Since 2020, we’ve seen Jalen Reagor, Derius Davis, Quentin Johnston, Savion Williams, and Jack Bech enter the league from there. There’s a very real chance none of them ultimately outperform their draft-capital-based B2S expectation. Eric McAlister could be next up in late April’s draft. Interestingly, though, he owns the second-best Breakout Score of that group, trailing only Quentin Johnston. And just barely — Johnston

name: CJ Daniels
blurb: My three-year-old son’s favorite wide receiver in this class is CJ Daniels. And that’s strictly because my son’s name is CJ. The ZAP Model does not share his enthusiasm. Daniels’ College Football Reference page reads like a 700-page novel. He spent four years at Liberty, where his only truly meaningful production came in Year 4. That season was legitimately impressive: a 3.14 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark — the fourth-best max-season number in this class — which helped fuel

name: Malik Benson
blurb: Malik Benson can absolutely run. The former track athlete clocked a 4.37 40 at the Combine, and when you watch him, the vertical juice is obvious. He’s built to threaten defenses over the top. The path to this point, though, has been anything but linear. Benson spent two seasons in JUCO before landing at Alabama in 2023, where he caught just 13 passes across 14 games. That led to a transfer to Florida State, where he managed only 25 receptions. He then finished his career at Oregon in 2025, his

name: Josh Cameron
blurb: Josh Cameron often looks like a running back playing wide receiver. He’s got a thick, sturdy build, and Baylor leaned into that across his five seasons there. He wasn’t used out of the backfield much, but he also wasn’t asked to win deep — his career deep-ball target rate was the second-lowest in this class. His average depth of target sat a bit higher because he lived in that intermediate range. Cameron is more of a traits bet than a production one. He’ll be past his 23rd birthday at draft time

name: Dillon Bell
blurb: The ZAP Model database now has six wide receivers with the last name Bell. Three of them are in this 2026 class. That’s analytics. Dillon Bell is generally viewed as the least exciting of the trio. He spent four seasons at Georgia — which is never a bad line on the résumé — but he never topped 466 receiving yards in a season. His best-year receiving yards per team pass attempt was under 1.0. Before this cycle, the ZAP Model had seen only nine wide receivers with a max-season mark that low. The o

name: Harrison Wallace
blurb: It’s tough to find an analytical hook in Harrison Wallace’s profile that screams upside. His Breakout Score sits below 30, he’ll already be 23 by draft day, his career yards per route run is just 1.86, and his best adjusted fantasy points per game season topped out at 12.7. He gained a first down on only 8.9% of his routes — an ordinary rate — and he doesn’t bring overwhelming size to compensate. Across the board, it’s hard to build a strong fantasy case. Wallace spent four years at Penn State b

name: Vinny Anthony
blurb: Vinny Anthony spent four years at Wisconsin and topped 400 receiving yards in just one of them. That junior-season spike was enough to get him to a 33.5 Breakout Score, but that’s about where the optimism peaks. From there, the profile gets shaky. Anthony’s best single-season yards per route run mark was a respectable 2.29, but the consistency and drive-ending impact just weren’t there. Anthony gained a first down on only 6.4% of his routes in college, the fourth-worst rate in this year’s class.

name: Aaron Anderson
blurb: There are four LSU wide receivers at the NFL Combine this year, but all of them are projected to go on Day 3 — including Aaron Anderson. Anderson began his career at Alabama, redshirting as a freshman before transferring to LSU. He then spent three seasons in Baton Rouge, where his production profile settled in as below average. His best adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt was 1.88, which translated to a 25.5 Breakout Score. That’s not moving the needle much analytically. To his credi

name: Jordan Hudson
blurb: Jordan Hudson was a highly recruited prospect who chose Texas Christian out of high school. He spent the 2022 season at TCU alongside a group that included NFLers Quentin Johnston, Derius Davis, Savion Williams, Jared Wiley, and Kendre Miller before transferring to SMU, where he played three seasons. The production at SMU was… fine. Nothing more. Hudson’s receiving yards per team pass attempt cleared 1.0 just once, and that came in 2025, his final year. He technically registered a Breakout Score

name: Jalen Walthall
blurb: No player in the ZAP Model database had come from Incarnate Word. Until now with Jalen Walthall. That’s where he spent two of his four collegiate seasons. The other two? Hawaii. What a wild, wild collegiate journey for a player looking to be drafted to the NFL. At Hawaii, Walthall’s production never got off the ground. As a freshman and sophomore, he posted sub-1.0 prorated receiving yards per team pass attempt rates. He appeared in just five games during his second year, but even on a full-seas

name: Eric Rivers
blurb: Eric Rivers owns the second-best single-season yards per route run mark in this class. Seriously. His career yards per route run sits at 2.40 — eighth-best among Combine invite wide receivers. So… do we have something here? Probably not. Rivers will turn 24 in September, putting him on the older side of the prospect spectrum. He played two years at Memphis with minimal production, transferred to Florida International for two more, and then finished at Georgia Tech. That eye-popping 3.57 yards pe

name: Jeff Caldwell
blurb: Jeff Caldwell might be the biggest size-speed outlier in this entire class. He’s 6’5”, 216 pounds, and ran a 4.31 at the Combine. On top of that, he posted the second-best broad jump and vertical among wide receiver invitees. That’s absurd movement at that frame. Those testing numbers probably shifted him from fringe-undrafted territory into “someone will take a shot” range. But Caldwell’s outlook is almost entirely traits-based. Caldwell began his career at Lindenwood in 2022, and he stayed the

name: Chase Roberts
blurb: The last time a BYU wide receiver was drafted was 2023. You might’ve heard of him — Puka Nacua. Those are pretty large shoes to fill for Chase Roberts. Let’s be clear, though: Roberts is almost certainly not the next Nacua. For starters, he’ll be 25 at draft time. In ZAP Model history, only four wide receivers have entered the league at 25 or older. The most notable recent example is Devaughn Vele — and even that’s more “solid depth piece” than fantasy cornerstone. Like Vele, Roberts is older la

name: Donaven McCulley
blurb: Donaven McCulley’s path to wide receiver is one of the more unique ones in this class. McCulley arrived at Indiana in 2021 as a quarterback. That experiment didn’t last long — he completed just 35 of 82 pass attempts as a freshman before making the switch to wide receiver. Per a USA Today piece, McCulley noted that he’d grown taller and always had good hands, so he made the switch. The athleticism was obvious. The position just needed to change. Year 2, his first as a receiver, was understandabl

name: Chris Hilton
blurb: All four LSU wide receivers invited to this year’s Combine checked in under 200 pounds — Aaron Anderson was the heaviest at just 191. Chris Hilton fits the mold, and with that lighter frame came good speed. He ran a 4.41 40-yard dash, which is a strong time. That’s about where the positives taper off. Hilton spent five seasons at LSU — credit to him for staying put in the portal era — but he never topped 250 receiving yards in a single year. As you’d expect, that means he failed to register a Br

name: Deion Burks
blurb: Projected draft capital is doing heavy lifting in Deion Burks’ profile, because the production doesn’t stand out. Burks spent three seasons at Purdue before transferring to Oklahoma for two more. Across that five-year career, his adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt never reached 2.0. He did manage a 34.6 Breakout Score, but that’s more middling than encouraging. The deeper efficiency numbers are even tougher. Burks posted a 1.35 career yards per route run rate, third-worst in this cla

name: Caullin Lacy
blurb: Caullin Lacy measured in at 5’9”, 183 pounds at the Combine and ran a 4.55 40. That translated to a 14th percentile Speed Score. In a wide receiver class loaded with high-end testers, that kind of number is going to hurt him on NFL boards. The saving grace is that, for someone this low in the ZAP Model, the production profile isn’t a total disaster. It’s just not especially inspiring, either. Lacy will turn 25 in December, making him one of the older prospects in the class. That’s the byproduct

name: J. Michael Sturdivant
blurb: We actually did see a flicker of early-career promise from J. Michael Sturdivant, the least user-friendly database name in the class (if you know, you know). Sturdivant began at Cal, and in his second season there, he posted an adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark that barely missed the (low) Breakout Score threshold. Unfortunately, that ended up being the high point of his collegiate journey in that metric, so he never officially recorded a Breakout Score. After Cal, Sturdivant t

name: Jeremiyah Love
blurb: We won’t get into the real-life ramifications of Arizona taking Jeremiyah Love third overall — this is a fantasy football guide. (But, man, that’s not what I would’ve done with that roster.) Love, as a player, is the truth. There aren’t really any red flags in his profile. His top-season prorated reception share of 12.4% could be better, but it still lands in the 65th percentile among ZAP Model running backs. His adjusted total yards per team play ranks in the 94th percentile, while his adjusted

name: Jadarian Price
blurb: Jadarian Price isn’t going to look great in any analytical prospect model. He played behind Jeremiyah Love in college, so his production profile is heavily suppressed — like, really suppressed. Price finished with a best-season reception share of just 2.8%. In ZAP Model history, 18 backs have fallen below the 3% threshold, and none became fantasy-relevant. Even if you expand that to backs below 5%, there are 46 of them, and only Derrick Henry produced a double-digit PPR points-per-game season wi

name: Jonah Coleman
blurb: Jonah Coleman is a true do-it-all back. He began his collegiate career at Arizona, logging over 200 combined carries across his freshman and sophomore seasons. After transferring to Washington for his final two years, he elevated his profile further. Coleman’s peak in total yards per team play came as a junior, though that season included a reception share below 8%. As a senior, he put it all together, posting a similar yards- per-team-play mark while nearly doubling his prorated reception share

name: Kaelon Black
blurb: For the first time in Late-Round Prospect Guide history, a profile page is being added post-draft. What a time to be alive. It’s not common for non-Combine invites to get drafted, let alone inside the top 100. But that’s what happened with Indiana running back Kaelon Black. San Francisco did the most Kyle Shanahan thing imaginable and took him with the 90th overall pick. Keep in mind, the running back ZAP Model bakes in some pre-draft projected capital, and Black was expected to go closer to Pic

name: Mike Washington
blurb: Mike Washington was the biggest faller at running back in this class. According to NFL Mock Draft Database, he was expected to go around Pick 63. Instead, he landed in Las Vegas at 122. Using Chase Stuart’s draft value chart, that drop equates to a 4.2-point gain for the Raiders. Since 2016, the only players with a similar gap between projected and actual capital are Kenneth Dixon, Dalvin Cook, Paul Perkins, Derrius Guice, and Damien Martinez. The fall isn’t all that surprising. Washington is a

name: Emmett Johnson
blurb: Emmett Johnson is one of my favorite prospects in this class, but he didn’t find the cleanest landing spot with the Chiefs. The long-term offense is appealing, sure, but Kenneth Walker’s arrival this offseason creates an immediate obstacle. The good news is that they’re very different backs. Walker wins with speed and explosive runs, while Johnson profiles more as a grinder — even at his size — with a strong receiving skill set. From a production standpoint, Johnson checks just about every box i

name: Nick Singleton
blurb: The Senior Bowl is invaluable for evaluating prospects up close. Unfortunately, injuries are part of the deal. That’s what happened to Penn State’s Nick Singleton, who broke his foot during practice in January. It may have contributed to his slide to Pick 165, where Tennessee scooped him up. According to the ZAP Model, that could be a value. Among Combine-invited running backs, Singleton ranks sixth in best-season prorated reception share, fifth in adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt,

name: Adam Randall
blurb: We seem to get a wide receiver-to-running back convert in every draft class. This year, it’s Adam Randall. The key difference between Randall and recent converts like Brashard Smith and Tyrone Tracy is size. Randall checks in north of 230 pounds. In ZAP Model history, he’s just one of 11 backs at that weight to post a best-season reception share above 10%. Most of the players in that bucket — Saquon Barkley, Leonard Fournette, Eddie Lacy, Jeremy Hill, James Conner, Najee Harris — came with stron

name: Eli Heidenreich
blurb: Chances are, as a seventh-round pick in a pretty throwaway landing spot, Eli Heidenreich won’t become anything meaningful for fantasy football. But, man, what a cool story and profile. Heidenreich went to Mt. Lebanon High School, right outside Pittsburgh. (I went to North Allegheny, also right outside Pittsburgh, but we won’t get into which high school is better. Now isn’t the time.) He then spent his college career at Navy. And, well, service academies don’t throw the ball. Since 2011, Army, Na

name: Demond Claiborne
blurb: You’ve seen it in other profiles, but for Day 3 running backs to hit, they often need to land in semi-ambiguous backfields. If their teammates aren’t being drafted highly in season-long formats, it’s a signal that there’s no clear starter. And where there’s uncertainty, there’s opportunity. Demond Claiborne lands in one of those situations in Minnesota. Size could be a limiting factor. At 5’10”, 188 pounds, the Wake Forest product handled a solid — but not dominant — workload across four seasons

name: Kaytron Allen
blurb: When you first look at Kaytron Allen’s production profile, the lack of receiving output stands out immediately. His best-season prorated reception share was just 8.6%, sixth-worst in the class, and his adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt was 0.50 — also sixth-worst. Allen spent all four seasons at Penn State alongside Nick Singleton, who was drafted just before him. Singleton handled more of the passing-game work, which naturally suppresses Allen’s receiving résumé in the model. Styli

name: Seth McGowan
blurb: Prior to this draft class, only one ZAP Model running back — Martez Carter — entered the NFL at age 25 or older. This year, we have two. One of them is Seth McGowan. Does age matter? Yes. The model accounts for it, and historically, older prospects have produced weaker outcomes. But context is needed. College football has shifted dramatically in recent years. Transfer rules changed. The pandemic extended eligibility. NIL deals altered incentives. As a result, we’re seeing older backs more freque

name: Jam Miller
blurb: Jam Miller flashed some juice at the NFL Combine. At 209 pounds, he ran a 4.42 40, giving him a Speed Score just shy of 110. That might be the best part of the profile, if we’re being honest. Miller spent four seasons at Alabama and never reached 150 carries in a year. His best rush attempt share season topped out at 29.7%. Through the air, he never hit an 8% reception share in any campaign. That combination — modest workload and minimal receiving — is historically tough to overcome. There are 3

name: Desmond Reid
blurb: Desmond Reid out of Pittsburgh (Hail 2 Pitt) isn’t just the smallest back in this year’s class — at 174 pounds, he’s the lightest running back in the ZAP Model database since 2017. That size is going to be the headline. And it should be. But if you zoom in on the production, it’s legitimately strong. Reid posted the second- best top-season reception share in the class at 20.4%. His adjusted receiving yards per team play also ranked second. And his best-season adjusted total yards per team play —

name: Roman Hemby
blurb: The advanced numbers don’t paint Roman Hemby as a dynamic runner. His career avoided tackles per rush rate is tied for the third-worst in this class. He gained 15 or more yards at the second-lowest rate. His yards after contact per attempt also sits in the bottom half among Combine invites. Those are rushing indicators. And they’re not flattering. The ZAP Model is a bit more forgiving because it leans heavily on receiving inputs. At Maryland, Hemby posted an 11% reception share or better in thre

name: Robert Henry
blurb: Robert Henry’s best-season reception share was 12.3%, which is respectable on the surface. The problem is that it came with major inefficiency, largely due to adjustments. In that same season, his age- and schedule-adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt was just 0.40, the fourth-worst mark in this class. Context doesn’t really save it, either. Henry played at Jones College and UTEP, and he reached higher than a 7% reception share in just that one campaign. He wasn’t facing elite weekly c

name: CJ Donaldson
blurb: When you weigh 230 pounds and rank dead last in your draft class in career missed tackles forced per attempt, that’s a red flag. That’s the starting point with CJ Donaldson. Among 220-plus-pound running backs since 2020, the only ones with missed tackles forced rates in his range are Gerrid Doaks and Stevie Scott. Not great! Donaldson spent three seasons at West Virginia before transferring to Ohio State for his final year. He was originally recruited as a tight end, which makes sense when you s

name: J’Mari Taylor
blurb: Back in 2020, J’Mari Taylor was a walk-on at North Carolina Central. He stayed there through 2024, steadily earning more work each season and eventually topping out with a 53% rush attempt share and an impressive 18.6% reception share in his final year. Taylor then transferred to Virginia for the 2025 season and continued to show pass- game ability, posting a 14.7% reception share against stronger competition. That’s an encouraging signal — the skill set translated upward in level. On the ground

name: Noah Whittington
blurb: Noah Whittington played six seasons of college football and never reached 1,000 total yards in a single year. Whittington’s most encouraging stretch likely came in Year 3, his first season at Oregon after two years at Western Kentucky. That year, he posted his best adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt rate and his second-best adjusted total yards per team play mark. He also tied his career-high reception share at 7.1%. And he did that in the same offense as Bucky Irving. You’d hope the

name: Le’Veon Moss
blurb: The issue with Le’Veon Moss isn’t the name — it absolutely sounds like an NFL running back’s name. It’s the production. Moss spent four years at Texas A&M and never once handled more than 25% of his team’s rush attempts in a season. His best-season attempt share was only higher than Eli Heidenreich’s in this class, and Heidenreich is basically a slot receiver playing running back. The receiving profile doesn’t bail Moss out. He posted just a 6.7% best-season reception share, the third-lowest mar

name: Rahsul Faison
blurb: Some prospects in this class took winding paths through college. Rahsul Faison went on a full-blown odyssey. First, the headline: Faison is 26 years old. He began his college career in 2019 at Marshall, transferred to Lackawanna College in 2020, then moved to Snow College, where he finally started seeing real playing time. After a couple of seasons there, he transferred to Utah State and spent two years with the Aggies. His best season came there, when he posted a 1.37 total yards per team play

name: Kenyon Sadiq
blurb: Kenyon Sadiq became just the seventh tight end drafted 16th or earlier in the NFL Draft since 2011, placing him in rarefied air. The others: Eric Ebron, TJ Hockenson, Kyle Pitts, Brock Bowers, Tyler Warren, and Colston Loveland. Each logged at least one season with 9.3 PPR points per game or more within their first three NFL seasons — a 90th- percentile max early-career season in the database at a minimum. Warren and Loveland have played just one season, too. This group also owns a 54% rate of t

name: Eli Stowers
blurb: Eli Stowers was getting steam entering the NFL Draft, and his consensus big board rank was nearing the first round by late April. He ended up going 54th overall, a little later than expected, which led to a larger draft capital differential between Stowers and Kenyon Sadiq than we may have anticipated. That’s relevant because, throughout the pre- draft process, Stowers and Sadiq became linked, as the former’s profile really started to shine. The buzz for Stowers really ramped up after the NFL Co

name: Max Klare
blurb: Despite being considered the TE3 on plenty of big boards entering the draft, Max Klare actually wound up as the TE5, going 61st overall to the Rams, who drafted a fun prospect in Terrance Ferguson (46th overall) just last year. Klare, though, is a fantasy football manager’s tight end. That is to say: he should be able to handle a lot of receiving volume — if it comes his way. Klare leads this year’s tight end class in Breakout Score, thanks to some big production at Purdue (specifically a 51-cat

name: Sam Roush
blurb: Despite being the 69th overall pick in the 2026 NFL Draft, Sam Roush was the sixth tight end off the board in what became a deep tight end draft class much quicker than we expected. The fit with the Bears, though, isn’t exactly ideal for Roush’s immediate fantasy football impact. Of course, Colston Loveland is looking like a stud after his rookie season, and Cole Kmet is under contract for two more years. Roush is huge, though, at 6’6” and 267 pounds, and the 4.70-second 40-yard dash at that siz

name: Marlin Klein
blurb: This is an unfathomable post-draft ZAP score swing for Marlin Klein, who was 164th on the consensus big board when the prospect guide was first published — fantasy afterthought territory as a projected late fifth-round pick. The Texans drafted him 59th overall. That’s a signal they’re willing to buy in on Klein as a developmental prospect, and it’s also basically earlier capital for a profile like his than we’ve almost ever seen (though we saw multiple reaches in the 2026 draft alone). The new t

name: Oscar Delp
blurb: Oscar Delp was selected 73rd overall by the New Orleans Saints, so he’ll be joining an older tight end room and have a chance to flash his athleticism in a dome for half of his games. That’s fun. Delp ran a 4.48-second 40-yard dash at his Georgia pro day, and even adjusting that for some potential pro day bias, he grades out with the second-best height-adjusted Speed Score in the class behind only Kenyon Sadiq. Delp’s production data is pretty weak, though. His max yards per team attempt and his

name: Eli Raridon
blurb: Eli Raridon was the ninth tight end drafted within the first 95 picks of the 2026 NFL Draft, which really put things into context. With 27 tight ends invited to the NFL Combine and most of them grading out well athletically, some of them were going to be drafted earlier than expected, and Raridon was another such case, vaulting up from a fifth-round projected pick in mid-March to being a third-round pick on draft day. Raridon played four seasons at Notre Dame, yet despite his size, he scored jus

name: Will Kacmarek
blurb: Will Kacmarek was projected to go undrafted when the prospect guide was first published. The Dolphins made him a third-round pick, 87th overall. This tight end class is something else, man. The class is highlighted primarily by two smaller, pass-catching tight ends in Kenyon Sadiq and Eli Stowers. Kacmarek is certainly not that. He weighed in at 261 pounds at the NFL Combine, making him the second-heaviest tight end in the class (of the 27 players invited). Kacmarek also ran a 4.74-second 40-yar

name: Matthew Hibner
blurb: After a big run on tight ends on Day 2, it took a while for the position to be called upon on Day 3. Matthew Hibner was the first tight end taken in Round 4 when he went 133rd overall to the Baltimore Ravens, joining a tight end room that is pretty barren after the team parted ways with Isaiah Likely in the offseason. Mark Andrews is locked up through the 2028 season, so we can’t gloss over that, but Durham Smythe’s contract is up after 2026. And that’s really it. So, there is a pretty immediate

name: Justin Joly
blurb: Justin Joly is a converted wide receiver with really strong age-adjusted production numbers (including an 88th-percentile Breakout Score among the tight end database). He was a borderline top-100 pick at various points in the pre-draft process but was finally taken off the board 152nd overall by the Denver Broncos. While the draft capital was lower than we’d like, it’s actually a solid landing spot in a tight end room led by Evan Engram and featuring just one name younger than 27 years old (Cale

name: Tanner Koziol
blurb: Tanner Koziol was the 12th tight end off the board in the 2026 NFL Draft and actually the second for the Jaguars, who selected blocking specialist Nate Boerkircher 56th overall. Koziol projects to be much more of a pass-catcher between the two, which is why the fantasy-focused ZAP Model likes him more than Boerkircher despite a massive difference in their draft capital. Koziol actually led two different offenses in receptions in his final two years of college, Ball State and Houston, respectivel

name: Joe Royer
blurb: Joe Royer was ranked as high as the TE2 by some analysts before the draft, and after a strong 2024 season, you’d see his name flirting with the first round in some early mock drafts. But after a step back in 2025, he was still on the board at pick 170 for the Cleveland Browns in a very unpredictable tight end class. Royer played five seasons in college, logging 38 total games. He appeared in 14 games over three seasons at Ohio State (including missing time due to the passing of his mother) befor

name: Nate Boerkircher
blurb: There were going to be some surprises at tight end this year in the draft. It was just bound to happen. Nate Boerkircher being selected as the third tight end off the board qualifies as one. Not only that, but it’s not like the position as a whole slid down the board. No, the Jaguars drafted him 56th overall. And, to be honest, the ZAP Model hasn’t handled many profiles like his before. Boerkircher is already 24 years old and will be 25 by the time the 2026 season starts. Only two other tight en

name: Bauer Sharp
blurb: A better-than-projected 40-yard dash time of 4.63 at the NFL Combine raised Bauer Sharp’s stock in the pre-draft model, but he was still a long shot and was projected to go undrafted at the time despite a 110 height-adjusted Speed Score. He still is a long shot, of course, but he was a sixth-round pick, 185th overall, by the Buccaneers, who were rumored to be interested in a tight end as early as the first round. That’s because their tight end room is pretty barren behind Cade Otton, who is unde

name: Riley Nowakowski
blurb: Riley Nowakowski feels like a Pittsburgh Steeler, and he became one with the 169th pick in the 2026 NFL Draft. But he doesn’t feel like a future fantasy football star. Nowakowski played six years in college: five of them at Wisconsin, and then his final year at Indiana. It took a long while for him to find his footing as a tight end, and he didn’t log more than 11 catches or 74 yards until his final season, when he caught 32 balls in 16 games for 387 yards (24.2 yards per game). Adjusting all of

name: Josh Cuevas
blurb: Josh Cuevas was the second tight end that the Baltimore Ravens drafted during the 2026 NFL Draft. They first took Southern Methodist’s Matthew Hibner (older, bigger, and more athletic than Cuevas) with pick 133 and then circled back to the position, grabbing Cuevas 40 picks later, 173rd overall. But it kind of makes sense for Cuevas to have an indirect path to an NFL team: he played for three different schools — Cal Poly, Washington, and Alabama — in five years. As you may expect, with a traject

name: Jack Endries
blurb: Jack Endries has a strong tie to the 2025 Heisman Trophy winner, Fernando Mendoza. They played two seasons together in 2023 and 2024 at Cal before transferring to different schools. Endries, as a redshirt sophomore, led the Golden Bears in receptions (56) and yards (623). But their draft capital couldn’t have been much different. Mendoza, of course, went first overall to the Raiders. Endries was selected 221st overall, in the seventh round, by the Bengals. He does possess solid age-adjusted prod

name: Jaren Kanak
blurb: Jaren Kanak has a really noteworthy profile. It wasn’t leading to much in the way of projected draft capital, but he did get selected. The Titans snagged Kanak in the seventh round, 225th overall. Kanak, a high school track athlete and dual-threat quarterback, was a four-star recruit as an athlete with interest from a lot of big programs. He ran a 4.52-second 40-yard dash at the NFL Combine, equating to a height-adjusted Speed Score of 109.2. While that’s not bad by any means, virtually every ti

name: John Michael Gyllenborg
blurb: John Michael Gyllenborg had some top-tier athletic testing, but we’ll circle back to that — because his profile probably needs some context first. After redshirting in 2021 (he didn’t even play football until his senior year in high school), Gyllenborg was at Wyoming for four more years, maxing out with a 30-catch, 425- yard, 3-touchdown season in 2024, all career highs (or matching a career high, in the case of touchdowns) in a low-volume passing offense. Adjusting his best season for age and s

name: Dallen Bentley
blurb: Dallen Bentley’s pre-draft big board rankings were all over the place, and he just barely got drafted, 256th overall, to the Denver Broncos. He was the second tight end the team drafted, after Justin Joly in the fifth round. Speaking of wide-ranging, let’s talk about Bentley’s college profile. Bentley didn’t draw much attention coming out of high school and did mission work before he played his first college ball at Snow College, where he didn’t do much (just eight catches in eight games). He th

name: RJ Maryland
blurb: Based on age- and schedule-adjusted data, RJ Maryland is one of the better receiving tight ends in this year’s class — and, frankly, in the entire tight end database. But you wouldn’t know it based on the pre-draft hype, which is why the ZAP Score and the comps are uninspiring. Maryland, the son of Russell Maryland (the first overall pick in the 1991 NFL Draft), is projected to go undrafted most places you look. Maryland tore his ACL during the 2024 season, ending with just seven games played. B

name: Michael Trigg
blurb: Michael Trigg’s profile is that of a well-rounded athlete. He played defensive end and basketball in high school and earned four-star recruiting status before heading to Southern California, the first of three NCAA schools for which he suited up. Trigg played only six games as a Trojan in 2021 and then seven and three games at Ole Miss in 2022 and 2023, respectively. Across those 16 games, he totaled just 28 catches for 330 yards and 5 touchdowns. But then at Baylor in 2024, Trigg notched 30 cat

name: Dae’Quan Wright
blurb: Dae’Quan Wright began his college career at Virginia Tech, making a bit of an impact (19 catches for 208 yards) as a true freshman in 2022. He then saw his role expand as a Hokie in 2023 but wound up transferring to Ole Miss and finding paydirt nine times in 28 games while being more involved as a receiver. Overall, Wright’s max yards per team attempt is a slight tick above the sample average, and his Breakout Score is about average. That’s not bad, especially if he’s as fast as his game tape ma

name: DJ Rogers
blurb: DJ Rogers isn’t really registering on the tight end model much at all. He played five years at TCU but didn’t catch more than 12 passes until his fifth and final year, when he caught 34 balls for 319 yards (9.4 yards per reception) and 2 touchdowns. In total, Rogers averaged under 1.4 yards per route run over his final three seasons, per PFF, and he was an underneath receiver by average depth of target, even after adjusting for team context. Despite that profile — which includes some size and at

name: Lake McRee
blurb: Lake McRee hits the NFL Draft process after five seasons at Southern California, ultimately finishing his career with 97 catches, 1,154 yards, and 7 touchdowns. He had a production jump in 2025, turning 30 catches into 450 yards and 4 touchdowns: all career-high outputs. Whether with low volume or high (again, a max of 30 catches), McRee did average at least 10.1 yards per catch in all five seasons, ending his college career with an 11.9-yard career yards per catch rate — and a 15.0-yard mark as

name: Miles Kitselman
blurb: Poor athletic testing was rare for tight ends at the Combine this year. Of course, only 20 of 27 Combine-invited tight ends ran the 40, but of those who did, only one ran slower than a 4.74: Miles Kitselman (4.90). That equates to an 88.2 height- adjusted Speed Score. That’s 12th-percentile in the tight end model. That doesn’t automatically mean there’s nothing in the profile to get excited about. Because even with that athleticism, Kitselman is drawing late-round draft projections, which makes

name: Khalil Dinkins
blurb: Khalil Dinkins is expected to go undrafted despite a 6’4”, 251-pound frame. Dinkins capped out at 14 catches and 167 yards in a single season at Penn State, and no matter how you slice the numbers, he’s toward the bottom of the tight end database in terms of adjusted production. He did post some good size-adjusted agility data at the Combine, but the explosiveness results were below-average, and the height-adjusted Speed Score (based on his 4.72-second 40-yard dash) is just about average. Dinkin
